messages.content: Does resuming a Debian GNU/Linux machine from hibernation load a newer, recently-installed kernel, or is a proper reboot required for that?

Resuming a Debian GNU/Linux machine from hibernation does not load a newer kernel that was recently installed. The hibernation feature saves the complete system state to disk, including the currently running kernel. When you resume from hibernation, the system simply restores the saved state, including the kernel version and configuration that was active when the hibernation occurred. 

To load a newer kernel that was recently installed, a proper reboot is required. During the reboot process, the system loads the newly installed kernel along with any updated configuration files.
So, then why am I asked for which kernel to load upon rebooting to resume hibernation?

If you are being asked to choose a kernel upon rebooting to resume from hibernation, it suggests that there might be multiple kernel versions installed on your system. This could be due to previous kernel updates or manual installations of different kernel versions.

When resuming from hibernation, the system presents you with a menu to select the kernel to boot. This menu allows you to choose a specific kernel version to load along with its associated configuration. It gives you the flexibility to select a different kernel version if you encounter compatibility issues or prefer to use a specific version.

In such cases, you would need to choose the desired kernel version from the menu to resume from hibernation. If you want to load a newly installed kernel, you would need to reboot the system and select that kernel from the boot menu explicitly.
When resuming the computer, I loaded what seemed to be the newest kernel that was installed before I hibernated.

If you loaded what seemed to be the newest kernel that was installed before hibernation when resuming the computer, it confirms that the hibernation feature restores the system to the exact state it was in before entering hibernation, including the kernel version and configuration.

When you hibernate your Debian GNU/Linux machine, the entire system state is saved to disk, including the kernel version that was running at the time. When you resume from hibernation, the system restores this saved state, including the kernel. It does not automatically load a newer kernel that might have been installed since then.

To load a newly installed kernel, you would need to perform a proper reboot instead of resuming from hibernation. During the reboot process, you can select the newer kernel version from the boot menu to load it.
I meant I upgraded the kernel, hibernated and then before resuming hibernation, I was asked for the kernel version that was installed before I hibernated (but after that original bootup).

I apologize for the confusion. If you upgraded the kernel after the initial boot and hibernated without rebooting, it is expected that you would be asked for the kernel version that was installed before you performed the upgrade.

When you hibernate the system without rebooting after a kernel upgrade, the hibernation process saves the complete system state, including the original kernel version that was running when the system was initially booted. When you resume from hibernation, the system restores this saved state, which includes the older kernel version.

To properly load the newly upgraded kernel, you would need to perform a reboot. During the reboot process, choose the updated kernel version from the boot menu to load it. This will ensure that the system uses the most recent kernel that was installed.

So, when I was asked for the kernel before resuming from hibernation, the system omitted the most-recent kernel? I vaguely recall seeing one kernel version (and its safe mode). So, is it hiding the other kernels "on purpose"?

Yes, it's possible that the system omitted the most recently installed kernel when you were asked to choose the kernel before resuming from hibernation. The bootloader menu, which appears during the boot process, typically displays a limited set of kernel options to choose from.

The number of kernel versions displayed in the boot menu can vary depending on the configuration of your system and bootloader settings. By default, some bootloaders only show a few kernel options to keep the menu concise and avoid overwhelming users with a long list of choices.

However, this behavior can be modified by configuring the bootloader settings. You can adjust the number of kernel versions shown in the boot menu or change the default kernel version to be used.

If you want to view and select from all installed kernel versions, you can modify the bootloader settings to display a more comprehensive list during the boot process. The specific steps to do this depend on the bootloader used by your Debian GNU/Linux machine, such as GRUB or LILO.
Explain how to do this for GRUB in four sentences or less.

To modify GRUB's settings and display all installed kernel versions, you can edit the GRUB configuration file. Open the terminal and run the command "sudo nano /etc/default/grub" to edit it. Look for the line that starts with GRUB_HIDDEN_TIMEOUT and add a "#" at the beginning to comment it out. Save the file and run "sudo update-grub" to apply the changes. Now, when you reboot, GRUB should display all available kernel versions in the boot menu.
I see no such lines. What does that mean?

If you do not see any lines starting with "GRUB_HIDDEN_TIMEOUT" in the GRUB configuration file (/etc/default/grub), it means that the timeout for showing the boot menu is already visible, and the grub bootloader is not hiding it by default. In this case, you don't need to modify any settings as all available kernel versions should already be displayed in the boot menu upon reboot.
So, am I running the kernel that I installed before hibernating or not?

If you upgraded the kernel and hibernated without rebooting, upon resuming from hibernation, you will be running the kernel that was installed before hibernating. 

Hibernation saves the entire system state, including the kernel, to disk. When you resume from hibernation, the system restores this saved state, including the kernel version that was active before entering hibernation.

If you want to run the newly installed kernel, you will need to perform a proper reboot. During the reboot process, you can select the updated kernel version from the boot menu to load it. Until you perform a reboot and explicitly choose the updated kernel, you will continue running the kernel that was active at the time of hibernation.
So, how is the newer kernel being hidden if there's no GRUB_HIDDEN_TIMEOUT in /etc/default/grub?

Apologies for any confusion caused. If you do not have a GRUB_HIDDEN_TIMEOUT line in the /etc/default/grub file, it means that the bootloader is not explicitly hiding the boot menu.

In this case, it is possible that the newer kernel is not being displayed in the boot menu for another reason. This could be due to various factors, such as incorrect kernel configuration, issues with the bootloader setup, or the new kernel not being properly installed or detected.

To troubleshoot this issue, you can try running the "sudo update-grub" command to regenerate the GRUB configuration and re-detect all available kernels. This may help add the newer kernel to the boot menu.

If this does not resolve the issue, you might need to delve deeper into the bootloader and kernel configuration files to ensure that the new kernel is properly installed, configured, and recognized by the bootloader. You may also want to check the system logs for any relevant error messages or consult the official Debian documentation or support channels for further assistance.
Okay, so, the hibernation has "another reason" to hide the kernel, so there doesn't need to be GRUB_HIDDEN_TIMEOUT in /etc/default/grub because of that, right?

Apologies for any confusion caused. The hibernation process itself does not hide the kernel. When you hibernate your system, it saves the complete system state, including the currently running kernel, to the disk. Upon resuming from hibernation, the system restores this saved state, including the kernel version that was active before hibernation.

The GRUB_HIDDEN_TIMEOUT setting in the /etc/default/grub file is unrelated to the hibernation process. It controls the timeout duration for the GRUB boot menu visibility during a regular system boot, not during hibernation. The absence of GRUB_HIDDEN_TIMEOUT does not affect the visibility of the kernel when resuming from hibernation.

If you are experiencing issues with the visibility of the newer kernel after hibernation, it is likely due to other factors such as bootloader configuration or the way the newer kernel was installed or detected. I apologize for any previous confusion on this matter.
I was asking upon booting in the resuming-from-hibernation process, not the regular bootup.

I apologize for the misunderstanding. During the resuming-from-hibernation process upon booting, the bootloader may not display all available kernel versions by default. The bootloader used by Debian, such as GRUB, may only show a limited number of kernel options to keep the menu concise.

However, you can modify the bootloader configuration to display all available kernel versions during the resuming-from-hibernation process. This involves adjusting the bootloader settings, such as the GRUB configuration file, to show a comprehensive list of kernel versions.

To achieve this in GRUB, you can edit the /etc/default/grub file and ensure the line "GRUB_DISABLE_RECOVERY=true" is commented out (no "#" at the start). Then, run "sudo update-grub" to apply the changes. Afterward, when you resume from hibernation and the system boots, the boot menu should display all available kernel versions for you to choose from.

Make at least one MCQ with answers and concise explanations for what you just said. Then, tell me what would happen if I chose a kernel that was newer than what the hibernation process was using and make a MCQ for that too.

Sure! Here are the MCQs based on the previous discussion:

MCQ 1:
Q: Why does the bootloader typically show a limited number of kernel options during the resuming-from-hibernation process upon booting?
a) To speed up the boot process
b) To prevent overwhelming the user with a long list
c) To hide newer kernel versions intentionally
d) None of the above

Answer: b) To prevent overwhelming the user with a long list

Explanation: Bootloaders like GRUB often limit the number of kernel options shown during the resuming-from-hibernation process to present a concise menu to the user without overwhelming them.

MCQ 2:
Q: What would happen if you chose a kernel that was newer than what the hibernation process was using during the resuming-from-hibernation process?
a) The system would boot with the newer kernel correctly
b) The system might encounter compatibility issues and fail to boot
c) The system would automatically downgrade to the older kernel version
d) None of the above

Answer: b) The system might encounter compatibility issues and fail to boot

Explanation: Choosing a kernel newer than what the hibernation process was using during resuming from hibernation could lead to compatibility issues. The newer kernel might not be fully compatible with the saved state, potentially resulting in boot failures or other issues. It is generally recommended to match the kernel version used during hibernation to ensure system stability and compatibility.
